generations.text:
My Dearest Eliza,

If you are reading these words, then I must be gone. Or perhaps you have come to visit, and I have been too foolish to see it. Either way, I write this with the ink of regret and the heart of a man who has long since lost the right to call himself a father.

Five years have passed since you left this house, and five years have been spent in the company of the sea, the wind, and the eternal vigil of the lighthouse. I have watched the horizon for storms, but none have ever come with such a weight upon my soul as the silence of your absence.

You were but a child when you left, and I, a man too stubborn to understand that love is not always seen in the arms of a father, but in the eyes of a daughter. I told you I would always be here, that I would stand by the light, and that you would always have a home. But I was wrong. I was a fool, and I was too proud to admit it.

The storm is coming. The wind howls like a beast in pain, and the sea roars as if it would swallow the very earth. I have lit the lamp, as I have done for many a night, but tonight feels different. I have seen the sky turn black, and I fear the light may not be enough to guide you home.

I do not know if you are still in the world, or if you have

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with a trembling hand, the wind howling like the wails of the damned, and the sea lashing against the cliffs as though it seeks to tear this world asunder. The lighthouse stands, though, and I—its keeper—stand with it. But I fear that when the storm is done, I may not be here to greet the dawn. And so, I must speak now, before the night swallows me whole.

Five years have passed since the last letter you sent me, and five years have been the longest of my life. I have kept the light burning, not just for the sailors who pass by, but for the memory of you, my daughter, the only light that ever truly guided me. I have heard the stories of your life, of your marriage, of your children, all through the whispers of the village and the occasional postcard. You have become a ghost in my mind, a shadow on the wall, and yet I have never forgotten you.

I have always believed that a father's duty is to protect, to guide, to be a beacon in the storm. But I have failed you, Eliza. I was too wrapped in the duties of this life, too lost in the solitude of the cliffs, to see the storm that was brewing in your heart. I was blind, as a man who has spent too long staring at the sea. I have not been there for you,

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you in better health and higher spirits than I have been in these past five years. I write with a heavy heart, for I have not spoken to you since the day you left our home, and the silence between us has grown longer than the sea at high tide.

Tonight, the skies darken with a storm that has not been seen in many a year. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the waves crash against the cliffs with a fury that shakes the very foundations of this lighthouse. I have spent my nights here, watching the tempest rage, and I have felt a strange kinship with the elements. The storm is not just a force of nature—it is a mirror, reflecting my own turmoil.

I have always believed in the light, you know. It is not merely the beacon that guides ships safely to shore, but a symbol of hope, of perseverance, and of the love that endures even in the darkest of times. Yet, I fear that the light I have been trying to shine for you has gone unheeded. I have tried to reach out, to call you back, but your silence has been deafening.

I remember the day you left. It was a quiet morning, the kind that promises peace, and yet it was the beginning of a storm in our hearts. You were so young, and I was so blind, not seeing the pain you carried or the sorrow that had

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like the wails of the sea itself. The sky is black as pitch, and the waves rise like the ghosts of those I have failed. I do not know if I will see another dawn, but if I do, I must tell you this—*I am sorry*.

Five years have passed since you left, and though the days have dragged like the tide, I have not forgotten you. You were once my light, my guiding star, and now I am but a lighthouse keeper, watching the world from a stone tower, my heart as hollow as the shell of this old lamp.

I know you think I have changed. That I have become a man of stone, unmoved by the storms of life. But I have not. I still dream of your laughter, still hear your voice in the wind, still feel the weight of your absence in every hour. I have tried to fill the silence with the work of the sea, but it is not enough. The sea is ever-changing, and so am I.

I have not written to you, nor have I sent a letter, because I feared what I might say. I feared that I would speak of love, of regret, of the mistakes I made. I feared that you would not hear me, or worse, that you would not want to. But now, as the storm gathers, I am forced to face the truth

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you in good health, though I dares not hope it finds you in my arms. Five years have passed since we last spoke, and though the distance between us has grown, my heart has only grown heavier with the weight of silence. I write to you now not from a place of anger or resentment, but from a place of deep, aching regret.

The storm that has gathered on the horizon is unlike any I have seen in my years of tending this lighthouse. The skies have darkened to a shade of black that even the most seasoned sailors would fear. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sea roars with a fury that shakes the very foundations of this tower. I have spent my life standing guard against the elements, but tonight, I fear I may not be able to stand against them.

You were always my light, Eleanor. Even when we were apart, even when the world seemed to have turned its back on us, I carried your memory like a beacon in the darkest of nights. I remember the way you used to stand on the shore, watching the waves with such wonder, and how you would ask me, "What happens when the light goes out, Papa?" I would tell you that even in the darkest night, the lighthouse would always remain, guiding the lost back to safety.

But now, as the storm approaches, I find myself wondering if I have been the one

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may find you in the same state of silence that has kept us apart for these five long years. I write to you now not with the hope of rekindling what was lost, but with the urgency of a man who feels the weight of the sea upon his shoulders and the shadow of the storm upon his heart.

The sky has darkened with an ominous hue, and the wind howls like a beast in the night. The sea, which has always been my companion, now roars with fury, and I can feel the very walls of this lighthouse trembling beneath its wrath. I have been here for many years, watching the tides and the stars, but never have I felt such a sense of vulnerability. The storm is not just a tempest of wind and water—it is a reminder of the fragility of life, and of the ties that bind us to one another.

I have often wondered, in the quiet hours of the night, what you might be doing. Are you still in the city, surrounded by the noise and bustle that you once found so exhilarating? Or have you found a quieter place, one where the heart can find peace? I cannot help but think of the days we spent together, the laughter, the dreams, and the unspoken words that lingered between us. I have carried them all these years, like the weight of a storm in my

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. Five long years have passed since the last time we spoke, and though I have often thought of you, I have never found the words to reach out. Tonight, as the wind howls through the cliffs and the sea roars in fury, I find myself at a loss for what to say. Perhaps it is the storm that has unmoored my tongue, or perhaps it is the weight of the years that have passed between us.

You were once a bright light in my life, a flame that burned with the promise of a future I could not yet see. But time, as cruel as the sea, has worn away the edges of our bond. I have watched you grow into a woman of your own will, and I have tried, in my own way, to guide you. Yet I fear I have only pushed you further away.

I do not know if I will survive this night. The lighthouse stands firm, but I am not so sure of my own strength. The storm is gathering, and the wind has already begun to tear at the very foundations of this place. I have lived here for many years, and I know the signs. This is no ordinary squall—this is a storm that will test the very limits of endurance. I pray the heavens will grant me the strength to see it through.

If I do not return, I beg you, do not let

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

**April 12th, 1887**

**The Lighthouse, Isle of Skye**

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you now, not with the hope of mending what has been broken, but with the weight of a man who has spent the last five years staring at the sea, waiting for the day the waves might carry my regrets to you. I know you will not read this, nor will you hear my voice, for you have long since turned your back on me. But I write, not to beg your forgiveness, but to tell you the truth—*before it is too late.*

The storm is coming. Not a mere squall, but a tempest of such fury that even the old stones of this lighthouse tremble beneath its wrath. The wind howls like a banshee, and the sea, that ever-hungry beast, has risen to meet it. I have lit the beacon, as I have done for thirty years, but tonight, I fear it may not be enough. I have spent my life keeping the light burning, but now, I find myself wondering if I have kept it for *myself* or for *you*.

You were but a child when you left this place, Eleanor. A child with eyes like the sea and a heart too proud to see the truth. You left with your mother's words on your lips, and I, in my

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands, for the wind howls like a beast outside, and the sea roars as if it would swallow the very stones of this lighthouse. I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet, here I am, writing to you as though you were still the child who once ran up the stairs to kiss my cheek before you left.

I do not know if I will survive this night, but I must try. The storm is fierce, and the light must be kept burning, for it is the only thing that keeps the sailors safe. I have always believed that the light is a promise—**a promise that even in the darkest hour, there is still a way forward.**

You left us, Eliza. I do not know why. You were once the light in my life, the daughter I held close, the one who made me believe that love could outlast the storm. But you walked away, and I followed, not in anger, but in sorrow. I have not spoken to you because I did not know how. I have not written to you because I feared what might come of it. But now, as the sea rages and the wind tears at the shutters, I feel the weight of silence pressing down on me.

I have not forgotten the day you left. I remember the way you stood at the door, your eyes full of tears, your voice steady but distant

**Letter from the Lighthouse Keeper**

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I sit here in the dim glow of the oil lamp, its flickering light casting long shadows across the stone walls of this old lighthouse. The wind howls outside, like a mournful spirit, and the sea roars with fury, as if it too is weeping. I have not spoken to you in five years, not since the day you left. Tonight, as the storm gathers strength, I find myself writing this letter—not to you, but to the child I once knew, the daughter I loved more than anything in this world.

You were my light, Eleanor. Not the light of the beacon, but the light that guided me through the darkest nights of my life. I remember the day you were born, the way your tiny hand gripped mine as you were laid upon the cold stone floor of our cottage. You were a miracle, a gift from God, and I vowed to protect you with all my heart.

But time, like the sea, is relentless. You grew, and so did I. The world changed, and with it, our paths diverged. I can still remember the last time we spoke, the way your voice trembled as you told me you couldn’t stay. You said you needed to find your own way, to be free. I didn’t understand then, and I still don’t. But I know now that you were right. You were always meant

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

**April 14, 1897**

**Lighthouse, Northumberland, England**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast outside these stone walls. The sea, once a faithful companion, now roars with fury, and I fear the night may not end kindly for us all. Yet, in this moment of peril, I find myself compelled to write, not only to you, but to the part of me that still remembers you as a child, wide-eyed and full of wonder, standing at the edge of the cliffs, asking me what the stars were made of.

Five years have passed since your last letter, and I have not heard from you since. I have tried to write, but the letters always seem to vanish—lost in the post, or perhaps, I fear, they were never sent. I do not know if you have forgotten me, or if you have chosen to forget me, and I cannot blame you. I have been a failure as a father, and I know it.

I have been a lighthouse keeper for over thirty years, and in that time, I have seen many storms, many lives lost to the sea. I have watched countless ships vanish into the dark, and I have stood alone at the top of this tower, guiding the way for those who would otherwise be lost. Yet, tonight, I feel the weight of the world upon me, and I

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I sit here by the window, the wind howling like a banshee, the sea roiling with a fury that makes my bones tremble. The lamps have been lit, the light shining through the glass like a beacon of hope in the darkness. But I wonder, as I always do, if there is anyone left to see it.

Five years, Eleanor. Five years since you left. Five years since I last saw your face, heard your voice, or felt the warmth of your presence. Time has not softened the ache of your absence. It has only deepened it.

I know you do not believe me when I say I have not forgotten you. That I have not forgotten the way your eyes would light up when you saw the lighthouse from the shore, or how you would run to the cliffs at dusk to watch the waves crash against the rocks. I know you think I have turned my back on you, that I have forgotten the girl you were, the daughter you once were.

But I have not forgotten. Not for a moment.

I have not forgotten the nights you stayed up with me, listening to the stories of the sea, or the way you would press your small hand into mine as we watched the storm rage outside. You were my light, Eleanor. And I have been a fool to let it fade.

I have not forgotten the day you left. I do not know what drove you, or what you thought you were

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write this with a quill that trembles, not from the cold, but from the weight of the words I must now utter. The storm is upon us, and though the lighthouse stands firm against the fury of the sea, I fear my heart may not endure the tempest of silence that has passed between us.

Five years have passed since you left, and though time has worn the edges of my sorrow, it has not softened the ache of your absence. I have kept the light burning each night, as I have always done, but I wonder now if the flame is more for the sea than for the land. For you, my child, I have kept it lit, hoping that somewhere, in the distance, your eyes might catch the glow and know that your father still watches over you.

I have heard the stories, Eleanor. I have heard the whispers of your life in the city, of your fine dresses and your fine manners, of the life you chose that was not mine. I have heard of the man you married, of the children you bore, of the life you built. And yet, I have not heard from you. Not once. Not even in the letters you once sent, which I kept, folded and tucked away in the drawer beneath the lighthouse's floorboards.

I do not blame you for what you did. I have no right to. You were a child, and I, a man who could not see the

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

**April 12th, 1889**

**The Keepers’ Cottage, White崖 Point**

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like the very devil himself outside these walls. The sea roars as if it would swallow the cliffs whole, and the lamp in the tower flickers with a feeble light. I have not seen the dawn in five years, nor have I seen your face. But tonight, as the storm gathers strength, I feel the weight of silence more keenly than ever.

Five years, Eleanor. Five years since you left this place, and I have not spoken a word to you. Not a single letter, not a single word. I have kept the lighthouse, tended the flame, and watched the world pass me by. I have watched the waves crash against the rocks, and I have watched the stars blink out one by one. But I have never watched you. Not once.

I do not know if I will see sunrise. The storm is unlike any I have known. The wind is not merely fierce—it is *alive*, as though the sea itself is angry. The sea, my Eleanor, has always been a cruel mistress, but tonight, I fear it may take more than just the light from the tower.

I have not spoken to you in five years, but I have thought of you every night. I have imagined your face

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may not find you at all. The winds howl like the ghosts of the sea, and the sky is a canvas of black and silver, as if the heavens themselves are weeping. I write this with a trembling hand, for I have not spoken to you in five long years, and yet, the weight of silence feels heavier than any storm.

I have been your father, and yet you have not been my daughter. We have lived in the shadow of our estrangement, each of us bound by the unspoken. I have tried, in my own way, to reach you, to mend the rift that has grown between us, but the distance—both physical and emotional—has proven too great. I have heard from your mother that you have taken up with a man named Charles, a merchant of some sort, and that you have settled in the city. I do not know if this is true, but I can only hope it is, for it gives me a thread to hold onto.

Tonight, the storm is upon us, and I fear for my life. The sea has been restless all week, and the wind has been a constant companion, howling through the cliffs and rattling the beams of the lighthouse. I have watched the waves rise like the walls of a fortress, and I have felt the tremors of the earth beneath my feet. I have always believed

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a quill that trembles in my hand, and a heart that feels as though it has been worn down by time, wind, and the weight of silence. Five years have passed since you left this house, and yet the echoes of your laughter still linger in the stone walls, as if they remember you better than I do.

You were but a child when you last stood in this room, your curls bouncing as you raced up the stairs to see the sea. I had told you that the lighthouse was not just a tower of stone, but a guardian of souls, a beacon for those lost in the dark. You believed me, as children do. But I fear I failed you in more ways than one.

I have not spoken to you in this time, not because I do not care, but because I feared my words would not be enough. I feared that the love I held for you would not be enough. I have been a man of routine, of duty, of the sea, and in my silence, I have lost sight of the daughter I once knew.

Tonight, a storm is coming—a storm like none I have seen in my years here. The wind already howls like a wounded beast, and the sea roars as though it would devour the very earth. I have spent my life watching the skies, reading the signs of the heavens, but tonight, I cannot read the fate that awaits me. I

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I sit by the flickering light of the lighthouse, the sea howling outside like a wounded beast. The wind whips through the cracks in the stone, and the waves crash against the rocks with a fury that makes my hands tremble. I've spent many a night here, watching the storm gather strength, but never have I felt so alone as I do tonight. The wind howls like a ghost, and I wonder if it carries your voice to me.

Five years have passed since you left, and I have not heard a word from you. I used to think that time would soften the edges of the pain, but it only deepens the ache. I've tried to write, but the letters always seem to vanish, as if the sea itself refuses to take them. I’ve even sent a few to your last known address, but they never arrived. I’ve often wondered if you never received them—or if you chose not to.

I have always been a man of the sea, Eleanor. My life has been shaped by its tides and tempests. But I never expected the storm to come from within. I've spent so many years watching over the harbor, guiding ships safely to shore, but I've never been able to guide you back to me. I’ve tried to be the father you needed, but perhaps I failed. I’ve tried to be the man you wanted, but perhaps I was not enough.

Tonight

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I sit by the flickering light of the lighthouse, the sea howling outside like a wounded beast, and I find myself longing for the warmth of your presence. Five years have passed since we last spoke, and I have not forgotten the words we exchanged—nor the silence that followed. The years have been heavy, and the distance between us has grown like the tide, ever rising and receding, yet never truly vanishing.

Tonight, the wind howls with a ferocity I have not known in many a year. The sea, once a companion, now roars with fury, as though it too feels the weight of our estrangement. I have watched the storm gather on the horizon, and I cannot shake the feeling that this night may be the last. The lighthouse, my sanctuary, has always been my guide, but tonight, it feels like a beacon of uncertainty.

I have always believed in the power of the sea, in its constancy, its rhythm, and its ability to endure. Yet, as I prepare for this storm, I find myself questioning whether I, too, can endure. The wind has grown so fierce that the light is nearly drowned out, and I fear that even the light may not be enough to guide those who pass by.

My Dearest Eliza,

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with the weight of years unspoken. Five long years have passed since you last called me father, and yet the silence between us has never felt so loud. I have watched the sea from these cliffs for decades, and I have learned that the greatest storms come not from the wind or the waves, but from the silence that lingers in the air.

Tonight, the sky has turned a shade of black that I have only seen in the aftermath of the worst tempests. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sea roars as though it would swallow the very cliffs that have sheltered me for so many years. I have stood here, alone, through countless storms, but never have I felt the weight of the world pressing upon me as I do now.

You were never meant to be here, Eliza. You were meant for a life beyond these stones, beyond the reach of the sea and the call of the lighthouse. I know you believe that I have kept you from the world, that I have bound you to this place with chains of my own making. But I have only ever wanted to protect you, to keep you safe from the cruel hands of fate. I thought that the sea, with all its fury and indifference, would be a safer place than the world beyond these walls.

But I have been wrong. And now, as the storm gathers strength, I wonder if

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with the weight of years unspoken. The wind howls like a wounded beast outside, and the sea, that ever-faithful companion, roars with fury. I fear the night may claim me before dawn, and thus I must speak, for I know not what tomorrow will bring.

Five years have passed since you left this house, and five years have passed since I last saw your face. I have not spoken of you to the others, nor have I dared to speak of you to myself. Yet here I am, pen in hand, writing to you as if the years have not yet stolen the right to call you my daughter.

I do not know if you will read this, nor do I know if you will believe the words that follow. But I write because I must. I write because I have carried this silence too long, and the storm has forced me to confront what I have long avoided.

You were not the first to leave me, Eleanor. You were not the first to walk away from the light. But you were the first to walk away from me.

I have been the lighthouse keeper for thirty years now, and I have watched the sea for more than half my life. I have seen ships vanish into the mist, I have seen the dawn break through the darkest of nights. I have seen the light endure, even when the world seemed to forget it. But

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you this evening, as the wind howls like a wounded beast and the sea roars with fury, as though the very heavens are crying out in protest. I have not spoken to you in five years, not a word, not a letter, not even a glance. And yet, as I stand here, alone in this tower, the weight of silence feels heavier than the storm that now threatens to consume us both.

I have always been a man of few words, but tonight, I find myself unable to hold my tongue. You are my daughter, my blood, my heart, and I have let you go. I have let you walk away, and I have let myself be forgotten. I have not written to you, nor have I sent a single letter, and I have not sought you out, even when the world around me turned its back on me.

But tonight is different. Tonight, the sea is not just a force of nature—it is a mirror, and in its depths, I see my own reflection. I see a man who has failed to protect what matters most. I see a father who has let fear and pride keep him from reaching out. And now, as the storm gathers, I am reminded that I may not survive this night. And if I do, I will not be the same.

I have spent years standing at this window, watching the world pass by, and I have been a fool. I

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a beast outside the lighthouse, rattling the shutters and shaking the very bones of this old place. The sea is restless tonight, and I fear it may not be the storm that claims me, but the silence that has grown between us.

Five years have passed since you left, Eleanor. Five long, lonely years. I've watched the sun rise and set, the moon wax and wane, and the tides come and go, but I have never seen a dawn as dark as the one that greeted you when you walked out of this house.

I know you don’t want to hear from me. You’ve made that clear. But tonight, as the sky darkens and the waves grow louder, I feel the weight of my silence pressing down on me like the sea itself. I can’t bear it much longer. I’ve spent my life keeping the light burning, and now I fear I’ve let the darkness in.

You were only ten when you left. I remember the day like it was yesterday. You came to me with your father’s old coat, the one he wore when he sailed the seas. You said you wanted to see the world, to find your own place, not be bound to this rock and this life. I tried to convince you otherwise. **The Lighthouse, Isle of Skye**  
**April 15th, 1889**

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like the wails of the damned, and the sea roiling as though it seeks to swallow the very cliffs that hold us. The storm is upon us, and I fear it may not be kind to this old frame. Yet I must write, for I have not spoken to you in five long years, and I would not leave this world without at least one final word.

You were always the light in my life, my dear Eleanor. I remember the day you were born—your cries like bells in the night, and your eyes, so wide and full of wonder. I used to think you would grow up to be the light of the world, and perhaps I was not entirely wrong. But time, as it often does, has a cruel way of taking what we love most.

I know you have not written to me. I have heard from your aunt, that you are now married, and that you have a child of your own. I have no wish to intrude upon your life, but I cannot help but wonder—does your son know of me? Does he know of his grandmother, who once stood on these cliffs, watching the sea with a lantern in her hand?

I have not seen you since the day you left, and I have not spoken to you since.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with the wind howling like a wounded beast outside the lighthouse, and the sea, that restless thing, roiling in fury as if it knows the hour is late. I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet tonight, as the storm gathers strength and I find myself alone with only the lamp and the sea for company, I feel the weight of silence pressing harder than the gale.

You were ten when you left. A child, with fire in your eyes and a heart too full for a girl of your years. I remember the last time I saw you, standing at the edge of the dock, your father's coat too big for you, your hands shaking as you clutched your small trunk. You looked at me as if I were a stranger, and perhaps I was. I had become a man of stone and salt, a keeper of light and shadow, and you, my daughter, had grown into a woman of your own making. I said nothing. I could not. I feared what words might do.

I have kept the lighthouse for twenty years now. The light burns every night, as it has since I took the post. My Dearest Eliza,

If you are reading these words, then perhaps I have not yet succumbed to the elements that now rage upon this coast, or perhaps you have already heard of the storm that has descended upon us with such fury. I write this in the dim glow of the lighthouse, the wind howling like the very devil himself, and the sea, in its wrath, pounding against the rocks with a sound that might be mistaken for the lamentations of the damned.

Five years have passed since we last spoke, and yet the weight of that silence sits heavy upon my soul. I have not written to you, nor have you written to me, and I daresay neither of us has been entirely honest in our reasons. But I must now, in this final hour, if it be the last, speak my truth.

You were a child when you left, and I, in my misguided pride, allowed it. I believed that the world beyond this lonely coast was one of opportunity, of grandeur, and of promise. I thought that if you could not find your place here, you would find it elsewhere. But I was wrong. You were not merely seeking a place in the world, my dear. You were seeking a place in the hearts of others, and when you found that you were not wanted, you turned your back on all of us.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I daresay it finds me in a state of unrest. The sea is restless tonight, and the wind howls like a banshee in the night. I have not slept in days, and the lantern’s light flickers with the storm’s fury. I write this with a trembling hand, for I fear I may not live to see the dawn.

Five years have passed since you left, and I have been a stranger to myself in your absence. I have kept the lighthouse, as I always have, but I have not kept your memory. It lingers in the salt air, in the creak of the beams, in the silence that falls when the waves are hushed. You were twelve when you left, Eleanor. I remember the day as if it were yesterday. You stood at the door, your face pale, your eyes red with tears, and said, *“I can’t stay here, Papa. **To My Dearest Eleanor,**

But now, as I stand at the edge of the cliff, the lighthouse beacon flickering in the dark, I feel the weight of those years more than ever.

I have been a lighthouse keeper for over thirty years, but I have never felt so lost as I do now. The light has always guided the ships, but tonight, it feels as though it cannot guide me. I have always believed in the light, in the power of hope, in the strength of love. But I have not been able to find it in myself for you.

You were my greatest joy, my light in the darkest nights. I remember the first time you came to visit, when you were just a child, your small hands gripping mine as we stood on the cliff, watching the sea.
